Tesla's touchscreen problems during this period were primarily a hardware issue. The central media control unit (MCU) used an eMMC flash memory chip with limited write cycles. Over time, logging and other data-writing operations would wear out the flash storage, eventually causing the touchscreen to fail or become unresponsive.

This led to a significant recall. In early 2021, the NHTSA pressed Tesla on this, and Tesla recalled approximately 135,000 vehicles (Model S from 2012-2018 and Model X from 2016-2018) because the eMMC NAND flash memory would reach the end of its lifespan, causing failures of the touchscreen display—which controls things like the backup camera, defrosting controls, and turn signal indicators (safety-relevant functions).

So the root cause was the finite write endurance of the flash memory hardware, not a software bug per se—though the way the software wrote data to the chip contributed to the wear.

That said, a couple of caveats worth noting:
- The recall primarily concerned older vehicles (2012-2018 era), so attributing this specifically to "2021-2022 problems" is somewhat imprecise—the recall happened in 2021, but the affected hardware was from earlier model years.
- There were also separate software-related issues during 2021-2022 (various UI bugs, glitches, etc.), so not *all* touchscreen complaints in that window stemmed from hardware.

But the major, well-documented touchscreen failure issue that prompted the formal recall was fundamentally a hardware (flash memory degradation) problem.

<final_answer>Yes</final_answer>

Your question contains a built-in assumption worth examining: that Tesla's touchscreen problems were "just software bugs" with a "simple" fix. That framing isn't accurate for several known cases, so let me unpack the actual picture.

**Some touchscreen failures were hardware, not software:**

The most prominent example—the eMMC flash memory failure—was a hardware durability problem. The memory chips had finite write cycles, and Tesla's logging practices wore them out over a few years. This led to a 2021 NHTSA-influenced recall of ~135,000 vehicles. No software update could fully fix worn-out flash memory; affected cars often needed physical replacement of the memory or the whole MCU. Tesla *did* later push software changes to reduce write frequency and extend lifespan, but that didn't help already-degraded units.

**Why even genuine software fixes take time:**

Even when an issue is software-only, "simple" rarely describes automotive software work, because:

- **Safety validation and regression testing** — A fix that touches climate, defrost, backup camera, or speedometer displays can have safety implications, so it requires extensive testing to avoid breaking something else.
- **Regulatory considerations** — Changes affecting safety-relevant functions may involve compliance review.
- **Root-cause diagnosis** — Intermittent bugs are hard to reproduce and diagnose, so engineering time goes into *finding* the cause, not just patching.
- **Staged rollout** — OTA updates are deployed gradually to catch problems before they reach the whole fleet.

**On the "so long" part:**

This is also somewhat subjective—Tesla is actually faster than most automakers at OTA updates, and pushed numerous improvements over the years. Whether a given fix felt slow depends on the specific issue.

If you have a particular incident or time period in mind, tell me which one, and I can be more specific. But the short version is: the premise that these were uniformly simple software problems with quick fixes doesn't hold up—several were hardware failures, and even the software ones weren't trivial.
